data.title: Celestica's Growth Story: Why It Still Makes Sense To Buy Despite High Valuation
data.description:
My first two articles on Celestica (CLS) share a similar thesis for investing in the stock, which centers on the company's transition from traditional contract manufacturing to an Original Design Manufacturer ("ODM"). This thesis has played out favorably for investors. The stock is up 92.63% from my first recommendation on January 14, 2025, compared to the S&P 500's (SPX) rise of 10.66%. It is also up 224.75% from my second recommendation on April 6, 2025, compared to the S&P 500's rise of 30.53% over the same period as of August 27, 2025. The stock is also up around 119% year-to-date.

The primary driver of Celestica's stock price increase is the company's Connectivity & Cloud Solutions ("CCS") segment, which is home to its ODM business model, enabling it to establish a solid position in the AI infrastructure market. It competes with companies like Cisco Systems (CSCO) in networking switches and Super Micro Computer (SMCI) and Dell Technologies (DELL) in building complete rack-scale AI system solutions.

The company has already begun attracting large hyperscalers to its services. It doesn't disclose its customers' names due to confidentiality reasons. However, the company has secured several design wins with large customers building AI infrastructure. Celestica's President and Chief Executive Officer ("CEO"), Rob Mionis, said on the company's fourth quarter 2024 earnings call:

On the networking front, we have been awarded our second 1.6T switching program with another large Hyperscaler customer, which will begin ramping in 2026. This HPS [Hardware Platform Solutions] program award includes supporting our customer with the design and production of a fully AI optimized networking rack, which will leverage our advanced system level cooling technology.

Celestica also announced a win for a new HPS program with a leading Digital Native Company. The CEO said the Digital Native Company was a "pioneer in the commercialization of AI [Artificial Intelligence] applications." He also added:

We will be collaborating with them [the Digital Native Company] to deliver a full rack, which is an optimized AI system solution built around the customer's custom ASIC accelerator. The program will leverage our proprietary R&D investments and develop expertise in AI/ML servers.

HPS is part of its CCS segment, focusing on the design, development, and manufacturing of hardware, including high-performance Ethernet networking switches, all-flash storage arrays, storage expansion platforms, high-performance servers and compute platforms, and rack integration services for customers' data centers. Celestica primarily uses an ODM model for most of the products made by the HPS portion of the company, which means that Celestica owns a significant part of the intellectual property ("IP") associated with building AI products for its customers. The products that the company manufactures using its proprietary IP have higher margins than its electronics manufacturing services ("EMS") model, where it uses its customers' IP.

The CCS segment, in addition to HPS, provides communications and enterprise customers with products using an EMS model. In other words, it will manufacture and assemble circuit boards, build complex electromechanical products, and combine subsystems into a final product for clients, all based on its customers' blueprints.

Bullish investors have driven the stock's price up over the last eight months, based on CCS and HPS revenue growth, which the company largely attributes to robust demand for its networking products, such as 400G and 800G Ethernet switches, placing the company in the sweet spot of the global AI supply chain. The HPS portion of the CCS segment has become a direct beneficiary of the massive capital expenditures by tech giants.

Celestica's second-quarter 2025 CCS segment revenue increased 28% year over year to $2.07 billion. The company's higher-margin and faster-growing HPS component of CCS increased revenue by 82% year-over-year, to approximately $1.2 billion.

Reasons for Investors to Continue Investing

Following the stock's run-up since April, some may question whether it remains a buy. Here are a few reasons that some remain bullish on the stock.

1. Market Leadership in AI Infrastructure:

The first reason to remain bullish on the stock aligns with a thesis I mentioned in my first and second articles on this company: Celestica's AI infrastructure business.

Celestica has positioned its ODM-focused HPS business as a "go-to partner" for hyperscalers building out AI and data center infrastructure. The company first emerged as a leading provider of 400 Gb Ethernet switches, a critical enabling component of high-speed, open networking solutions for AI, machine learning (ML), and cloud computing. Although the company has big-name competitors in Ethernet switches, such as Arista Networks (ANET) and Cisco Systems, some hyperscalers and other players prefer to partner with Celestica because they gain greater say in the design and development of the switches. Hyperscalers prefer using customized hardware that is optimized for their unique data center architecture, power usage, and software environments.

In contrast, Arista Networks' and Cisco Systems' bread and butter is selling proprietary, off-the-shelf, "one size fits all" hardware and software solutions. Therefore, Celestica's ODM approach provides a competitive advantage over some of its larger, more well-known competitors, as long as it remains at the cutting edge of the latest technology. Currently, the cutting edge in Ethernet switches is 800 G, which offers twice the bandwidth of the previous 400 G standard, along with a 75% reduction in power consumption. The higher the bandwidth, the faster a customer can process their data at ultra-low latency, and the faster that hyperscalers can perform data-intensive activities, such as AI training.

Celestica began selling its first 800G Ethernet switch, the DS5000, on October 16, 2023, one of the first companies to launch switching solutions for 800G Ethernet switches, quickly becoming a market share leader and cementing its position as a top vendor in the data center switch market with its growing market share in 800G. CEO Mionis said on the company's second quarter 2025 earnings call:

Our market share also for 800G is that much larger than market share for 400G as well based on our early wins. So the breadth that we're seeing across a number of hyperscalers and the ramps we're seeing across a number of hyperscalers is great to see, and it's fairly pronounced.

Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Mandeep Chawla added:

The 400G demand has been very strong now for a quite some time. We saw a lot of strength in the first quarter. What was nice about the second quarter is the 800G now is ramping and it's basically on parity with our 400G volumes in the second quarter. And now we see 800G continuing to accelerate. So the point that Rob made, if you think about our top 3 hyperscaler customers, we're -- we've won 800G programs with all 3 of them.

Securing wins with three top hyperscalers is evidence of a solid competitive moat. The company is no longer solely a low-margin contract manufacturer like it was up until the early 2000s, but a key strategic partner with valuable IP to bring to the table with its customers. As the company shifts more towards its ODM business model, investors will likely award the stock a higher valuation.

Dell'Oro Group published an article on June 5, 2025, stating:

According to a recently published report from Dell'Oro Group, the trusted source for market information about the telecommunications, security, networks, and data center industries, Ethernet Data Center Switch sales surged over 40 percent in the first quarter of 2025, marking the strongest growth since we began separately tracking the market in 2013 and breaking a new all-time high. Celestica and NVIDIA captured the lion's share of the gains, each expanding their revenue share by more than five percentage points.

Market researcher for IT, Cloud, communications, and telecommunications firm 650 Group published an article stating:

Scale-out infrastructure is rapidly moving towards Ethernet and is the key driver of 800G growth in 2025. While InfiniBand remains a key technology, by the end of 2025, Ethernet will be the dominant technology for Scale-out, and we will start to see the early 1.6T ramp. For 2025, Scale-out networking will grow over 100% and exceed $8B in revenue (no optics). For Ethernet in 2024, the top 3 vendors for scale-out backend are (Celestica, NVIDIA, and Arista).

AI infrastructure companies utilize scale-out switches to connect additional resources, such as servers, storage devices, or processing units, within a single network. As long as Celestica remains competitive enough to compete with heavyweights like NVIDIA (NVDA) and Arista for backend scale-out AI infrastructure and industry analysts acknowledge it as a leader, it has a bright future, provided hyperscalers continue to be interested in building out AI infrastructure.

2. The Shift to Higher-Value Business

The potential for Celestica to improve long-term operating margins through its ODM and services businesses is a significant reason to continue investing in the stock. The company has historically been a contract manufacturer with thin margins. Over time, the company's strategy has evolved to move up the value chain, offering higher-value products and services. The company's strategic shift to a higher-margin ODM model within its CCS segment gained momentum with the surge in demand for AI infrastructure in early 2023. You can see the shift to its ODM business model within its operating margin improvement. Its second-quarter 2025 CCS segment margin increased 1.3 points year over year to 8.3%.

Furthermore, the company expects its expansion into high-margin services within the Advanced Technology Solutions ("ATS") segment to also be a long-term driver of profitability. The ATS segment margins increased 70 basis points ("bps") year over year to 5.3%. CEO Mionis stated the following on the second-quarter earnings call: "In our ATS segment, solid demand in our capital equipment business and industrial businesses drove higher-than-expected revenues, while segment margins of 5.3% continue to improve meaningfully." The company heavily focuses on profitability, not growth for the sake of growth.

An example of Celestica's focus on improving profitability is management's decision to exit a margin-dilutive program within its Aerospace & Defense (A&D) business (part of the ATS segment). Management made the decision to exit a program to improve overall profitability, even if it meant incurring some revenue loss. Instead of focusing on non-profitable areas, the company continues to invest in potentially high-value, high-margin projects.

The company seeks these high-margin projects within a range of collaborative design and engineering services, as well as Supply Chain and Logistics Services. It also offers several aftermarket services, including repair and refurbishment, as well as product licensing and asset management.

Before its strategic direction change within its CCS segment to an ODM model, Celestica's ATS segment had historically been the higher-margin area of the company's business compared to its CCS segment. Although the CCS segment is now its higher-margin growth engine, the ATS segment, a provider of services for Aerospace & Defense (A&D), Capital Equipment, HealthTech, and Industrial businesses, offers a good mix of countercyclical businesses that may cushion against a potential cyclical downturn in the AI infrastructure industry.

The A&D business, driven by long-term government contracts and HealthTech, where the demand for medical devices, services, and treatments is not closely tied to economic prosperity, is exceptionally resistant to the cyclicality of the AI infrastructure business. Therefore, the ATS segment can also provide valuable diversification, potentially blunting adverse impacts on margins during an AI infrastructure industry downturn.

Investors should look for the company's strategies to yield higher margins over time and drive rapid bottom-line growth. Its rising margins resulted in second quarter 2025 diluted GAAP earnings per share ("EPS") increasing 128% year over year to $1.82, beating analysts' estimates by $0.72.

3. Strategic R&D and Early Wins on New Technology

The company's specific R&D investment focus area should maintain its leadership position in next-generation technology. While the company is currently ramping up its cutting-edge 800G Ethernet technology, it is also achieving early success in developing 1.6-terabit (1.6T) networking switches. Celestica has already secured contracts for these next-generation networking switches. CEO Mionis said on the second-quarter earnings call:

We received [Broadcom's (AVGO)] Tomahawk 6 samples in June [2025], and we successfully brought up the first system within days of receiving the sample. So that bodes well for the silicon and bodes well for our engineering. Right now, we have several new programs one Tomahawk 6 programs, 1.6T programs that will start generating some revenue in the back half of 2026 and certainly into 2027. Again, this will all be paced by silicon availability.

Tomahawk 6 is Broadcom's latest and fastest networking chip. Celestica was able to get its system with Tomahawk 6 up and running quickly, which is evidence of its strong relationship and its ability to collaborate with Broadcom. The company has a head start on the competition in 1.6T Ethernet switches, which is a potential competitive advantage in gaining design wins for switches. As the above quote highlights, management expects to begin generating revenue from 1.6T Ethernet programs as early as late 2026.

Celestica's investment in AI server systems is also starting to pay off. It is grabbing market share from competitors, an indication of a solid competitive advantage in the high-growth AI server market. CEO Mionis said on the second-quarter earnings call:

We are gaining share with our largest customers with respect to AI server market share. Frankly, a lot of that is just due to strong execution and ability to build these very complex products at scale. And as [CFO] Mandeep mentioned, we just went through a technology transition. We see these programs starting to ramp in the third quarter and gaining some significant momentum as we exit the year, and also into next year. And we also expect this product line to produce probably even more revenues based on that increased share as we get into late '26 and into '27 and beyond based on next-generation programs.

The company has also utilized its R&D to develop proprietary designs for modular AI/ML compute platforms where all hardware components and their configurations are automatically managed and controlled by software, acting as a single, unified system (a fully orchestrated rack). This platform is not just a generic off-the-shelf server, but a complete, ready-to-deploy system optimized for AI workloads, often featuring advanced cooling systems to manage the intense heat generated by AI chips. The "digital native win" I mentioned earlier in this article refers to Celestica providing a fully orchestrated rack to a high-profile customer, which will serve as a proof of concept for its modular AI/ML compute platform for other potential customers, including hyperscalers.

CEO Mionis said on the earnings call:

In terms of the digital native win and doing that fully orchestrated rack, that is certainly opening up new doors and new conversations with people, even the hyperscalers. But the entry point on that might be next-generation systems in terms of what more can we do. So those conversations are still, I'll call it, in the early stages, but producing a lot of interesting conversations.

The company's early design wins in next-generation AI infrastructure technologies are evidence that Celestica's R&D programs are providing it with a first-mover competitive advantage, thereby maintaining its leadership position in AI infrastructure.

Valuation

Celestica's trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 2.2, well above its three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year medians, suggesting overvaluation.

However, using historical medians may not be the best way to assess its valuation, as the historical P/S ratios include periods when it was more heavily weighted toward traditional contract manufacturing services. Once demand for AI infrastructure began rising in late 2022 and early 2023, Celestica's AI infrastructure business and its ODM model took off. The company's negative revenue growth in 2019 turned positive by 2022. By January 2023, Celestica's revenue growth had peaked at over 30% year-over-year. The market's rising P/S ratio over the last two years reflects the company's transition to a higher-margin ODM model and positive revenue growth, rendering a simple comparison to historical P/S medians as moot.

The following table compares Celestica's one-year forward P/S, one-year forward P/S/G, and TTM profitability metrics.

Company Name One-year forward P/S ratio one-year forward estimated revenue growth one-year forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) TTM FCF margin TTM operating margin TTM Gross margin TTM Return on invested capital [ROIC]
Celestica 1.90 20.72% 0.09 3.72% 8.32% 11.37% 19.85%
Supermicro 0.82 45.30% 0.02 6.97% 5.70% 11.06% 11.92%
Dell Technologies 0.85 9.70% 0.09 3.77% 7.23% 21.73% 18.57%
Hewlett-Packard Enterprises (HPE) 0.87 13.46% 0.06 0.34% 6.70% 29.19% 3.71%
Jabil (JBL) 0.79 0.37% 2.13 4.0% 4.91% 8.89% 11.46%
Flex (FLEX) 0.77 3.28% 0.23 4.25% 5.28% 8.82% 9.76%
Sanmina (SANM) 0.79 6.80% 0.12 4.52% 4.70% 8.65% 10.15%

Investors may consider Celestica to be undervalued, fairly valued, or overvalued, depending on the valuation metrics they prefer to use. Celestica's P/S ratio (1.90) is at a premium valuation to its competitors. Some may consider the company overvalued based on its P/S ratio.

However, Celestica's P/S/G ratio is 0.09, which is slightly higher than Dell's (0.09), Supermicro's (0.02), and HPE's (0.06), but significantly lower than Jabil's (2.13), Flex's (0.23), and Sanmina's (0.12). Some consider a lower P/S/G ratio as a sign that the market undervalues the stock relative to its peers. As a result, investors may not be able to use the P/S/G ratio to definitely determine whether it is overvalued or undervalued compared to peers.

Still, including the company's valuation metrics in the comparison may clarify things. The company's relatively high operating margin and ROIC may justify Celestica's premium P/S valuation, and some investors may consider the stock undervalued/fairly valued based on its P/S/G ratio and its profitability.

The company's forward EV-to-EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) ratio of 24.16 is well above its peers, suggesting overvaluation, until one considers the comparison of the companies' EBITDA growth rates.

Celestica's second-quarter 2025 year-over-year EBITDA growth rate of 123.1% may justify its premium forward EV-to-EBITDA valuation.

Still, let's compare valuations using a forward-looking perspective. The following chart indicates that analysts expect Celestica to increase its EBITDA at a 20.28% compounded annual growth rate ("CAGR) from $770.60 million in 2024 to $1.344 billion in 2027.

I sourced the following EBITDA estimates from YCharts and calculated the EBITDA CAGR using a CAGR calculator, as shown in the following table. According to my calculations, Celestica has the second-highest EBITDA CAGR to 2027, exceeded only by Super Micro, which justifies Celestica's premium EV-to-EBITDA valuation over all its peers, except Super Micro.

Company name Estimated EBITDA CAGR to 2027
Celestica 20.28%
Super Micro Computer 34.25%
Dell 12.07%
Jabil 9.05%
Flex 11.05%
Sanmina 7.97%

Celestica's forward price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 35.67 is above the median of 30.34 for the Information Technology sector. Therefore, some may consider the stock overvalued or fairly valued.

The company's 2025 forward P/E to year-over-year EPS growth rate ("PEG") ratio is 0.82. Generally, the market considers PEG ratios below 1.0 as undervalued. Growth investors may allow the PEG ratio to rise as high as 2.0.

Suppose Celestica currently trades at a 2025 PEG ratio of 1.0; its stock price would be $242.35, a 15% rise over its August 28 intraday price of $210.29. If it trades at a 2025 PEG ratio of 2.0, its price would be $484.70, representing a 130% increase over its intraday price of August 28. However, investors should not expect it to reach a 2025 PEG ratio of 2.0 because of the discount that the market likely applies to the company's CMS portion of its business.

Investors should monitor whether the HPS portion of its business continues to outgrow the overall CCS segment, which would indicate a shift in the mix from the lower-margin CMS portion to the higher-margin ODM portion of its business. Investors may award Celestica higher PEG ratios if they observe that the mix continues to shift toward higher-margin ATS services and the higher-margin ODM portion of its business.

The company's price-to-FCF (P/FCF) of 63.02 is well above its three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year medians, either indicating overvaluation or that the market awarded the company a premium P/FCF valuation due to a belief that FCF will grow rapidly in the future and the stock will "grow into its valuation."

The company's rapidly growing operating margin is a potential indicator that FCF margins will also expand in the future, although it doesn't guarantee it. Celestica's FCF margin was 2.71% in 2024, and analysts expect it to grow to 3.58%, 3.62%, and 3.63% in 2025, 2026, and 2027, respectively.

Risks

Celestica's most significant risk is its dependence on a small number of customers. The company's 2024 10-K stated:

We depend (and expect to continue to depend) upon a small number of customers for a substantial portion of our revenue. In the aggregate, our top 10 customers represented 73% of total revenue for 2024, 64% for 2023 and 66% for 2022.

Suppose Celestica loses the business of one or more significant customers; it could result in a decline in revenue, operating results, FCF, and its financial position. The market would likely sell off the stock once investors discover a customer is reducing its business with Celestica. Investors should monitor news of hyperscalers' capital investment plans. News of any one customer reducing its AI capital expenditures may send shock waves throughout the AI infrastructure industry and cause a selloff.

That comment also highlights the company's significant reliance on a single industry, AI infrastructure. Although the current AI boom is a massive tailwind for revenue growth, it also represents an industry concentration risk. A significant portion of Celestica's revenue growth comes from demand from a single, rapidly growing end-market. If the AI infrastructure industry experiences a downturn, it would adversely impact the company's revenue and earnings growth.

It is also heavily dependent on its suppliers. For instance, if Broadcom is unable to deliver its Tomahawk switches to Celestica promptly, its ability to provide its Ethernet switches to customers would decline, which would adversely impact revenue growth. The market would likely lower the stock's valuation in response, resulting in a selloff.

I maintain a buy for Celestica

Value investors may not be pleased with the company's valuation following the stock's recent surge since April. As a result, investors concerned with the company's valuation or near-term upside should avoid investing in the stock. The valuation is approaching levels where even I am considering downgrading it to a hold if the stock price increases without a corresponding improvement in fundamentals. However, if you believe it can still tap into more revenue and earnings growth potential, this stock may still be on your list of potential buys.

One blog developed a range of AI infrastructure market sizes by assessing the opinions of several prominent industry analysts, such as Market.us and MarketsandMarkets. That blogging website's compilation of estimates for the AI infrastructure market suggests an AI infrastructure addressable market size of $38.1 billion to $135.81 billion by 2025.

The most significant, fastest-growing, highest margin portion of Celestica's business is HPS, which serves as a rough proxy for the AI infrastructure part of its business. HPS generated TTM revenue of $3.761 billion as of the second quarter of 2025, penetrating 2.76% to 9.89% of a credible addressable market size for the AI infrastructure market. The same blog forecasts that the AI infrastructure market will reach $499.33 billion globally by 2034. The company's second-quarter 2025 HPS TAM has only penetrated 0.75% of a $499.33 billion 2034 addressable market, indicating that Celestica's HPS portion of its business has substantial room to grow. Suppose the company can also continue to increase margins in its ATS business by expanding its services. In that case, it will enhance the company's long-term profitability and strengthen the countercyclical part of its business.

Although several valuation metrics flash yellow or red, if the company's PEG ratio rises to fair value, the stock would increase by 15% over its intraday price of August 28. Suppose the company continues expanding its operating margins; the market may be willing to award it a higher PEG ratio, and the stock price may still have further upside. If you are a growth investor, this stock may continue to attract your interest. I maintain my buy rating on the stock for now.

data.title: Morgan Stanley Direct Lending: Consistent Performer With A Rockbottom Valuation
data.description:
In this article we take a look at the Q2 results for the BDC Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (NYSE:MSDL). MSDL falls under the $18bn Morgan Stanley private credit platform which itself sits under the $1.5trn Morgan Stanley Investment Management. We last covered MSDL here.

MSDL trades at an 11.3% dividend yield - about 0.5% below the median in our coverage with the same net investment income yield. MSDL delivered a 2.2% total NAV return for the quarter, below the median but above the average. On a valuation-adjusted basis, it is on par with the median.

Much like the rest of the BDC space, MSDL allocates primarily to less-cyclical sectors such as Software, Insurance and Commercial Services. It is somewhat underweight Healthcare, a sector which is often one of the top two sector weights for BDCs. It is also underweight trade-sensitive areas such as consumer goods and manufacturing.

The company just appointed Michael Occi as the new CEO. Occi was previously the President of MSDL and has been with the firm for two decades and we don't expect this appointment to be a material change for the company.

Quarter Update

Net investment income came in at $0.5 - a drop of about 3.8% from Q1 levels. This was due to continued spread compression - a common theme in the sector where NII has fallen 3.4% on a median basis in Q2.

As we mentioned in our previous updates, we expected coverage to test 100% and we are there now.

The company declared the same $0.50 base dividend. It will be tested if the Fed restarts its rate-cutting cycle.

The NAV fell slightly due to unrealized depreciation.

The NAV has been stable over the last several years.

Management mentioned that deal activity remained subdued while lending spreads compressed by around 0.25% relative to Q1 - a common theme we hear from other managers. Management also believes that the market is close to the trough in spreads which should lessen the downward pressure on NII over the medium term, all else equal.

MSDL has a 10b5-1 share repurchase plan in place which kicks into gear when the stock trades below the NAV with larger volumes repurchased at wider discounts. Year-to-date, $30m has been bought back out of the $100m capacity. Full capacity represents around 6.5% of the market cap. A share repurchase plan is always nice to see as it's the easiest way for management to add shareholder value via NAV accretion.

Income Dynamics

Net new investments were flat for the second quarter running.

Leverage increased slightly as the share count fell by around 1.4%. The company's leverage level is in line with the median in our coverage. Management said the sweet spot is around 1.15-1.2x which provides potential dry powder in case spreads widen and the lending environment improves.

The portfolio asset yield fell slightly. This has been a common pattern in Q2 as tight lending spreads and portfolio turnover are pushing the overall portfolio yield lower despite stable short-term rates. Interest expense fell to 6%, in line with the median in our coverage.

MSDL refinanced its 2025 7.75% bond with a 6% 2030 issue, materially lowering its overall interest expense. As we discussed in a recent weekly, BDCs are taking advantage of very tight credit spreads to refinance bonds at attractive coupons despite elevated Treasury yields.

The company also priced its inaugural CLO. This not only helps it to diversify its liabilities but also should continue to drive down its interest expense as CLOs tend to be cheaper than credit facilities while also placing fewer leverage constraints on the company.

The company's 9.7% NII NAV yield is on the low end of the 10.5% median in our coverage, though in line with other larger BDCs like ARCC and OCSL. However, its low management fee and wide discount partly close the gap between it and the sector on a price basis.

Portfolio Quality

Non-accruals on fair-value rose to 0.7% at cost which compares very favorably to the 2.5% median in our coverage (and 3.9% average). We typically only quote fair-value figures, however, MSDL prefer to disclose the at-cost figures instead, which is arguably a better indicator of ongoing credit issues. This is because new non-accruals can be quickly written down, presenting a low headline figure (though the stress would still show up in unrealized depreciation).

Portfolio quality, as gauged by internal ratings, improved slightly as the allocation in the two lowest-quality buckets declined.

There was a small net realized gain.

The allocation to first-lien loans is one of the highest in the sector and well above the 85% sector median.

Performance and Valuation

MSDL continues to outperform the broader BDC sector in our coverage over various time horizons.

The level of outperformance has fallen more recently. This is due in part to the end of fee waivers and, perhaps, a good credit environment where the company's higher-quality portfolio is more of a drag than a tailwind due to lower spreads.

MSDL is trading near its valuation lows.

It is at a double-digit discount relative to the sector and looks unusually cheap.

The stock looks very attractive on a valuation-adjusted basis. And although forward performance may come in below its historical level due to the end of the fee waivers, it still looks very compelling.

Stance and Takeaways

The company's low management fee of 1% and wide discount allow it to generate a level of earnings yield not far off the median BDC in our coverage despite a senior focus with higher-quality lending and modest leverage. A large and diversified portfolio, a focus on first-lien underwriting and a consistently low level of non-accruals evidence a high-quality underwriting process. The stock's valuation is unusually cheap for its history and its level of performance and we view it as very attractive.

data.title: EQT: Benefiting From Natural Gas Fundamentals
data.description:
EQT Corporation (NYSE:EQT) has already seen substantial YoY improvement in its share price as the company has benefited from growing natural gas demand. Despite that, the company is continuing to realize higher assets, grow its portfolio, and decrease its breakeven. That will enable the company to drive substantial shareholder returns.

2Q 2025 Results

EQT Corporation had 568 Bcfe sales in the quarter at $2.81 / Mcfe.

The company has continued to have strong operations at $1.08 per Mcfe with $1.03 billion in adjusted EBITDA. After manageable capital expenditures, the company had $240 million in FCF. That's almost $1 billion annualized in FCF, putting the company at a 3% FCF yield, although the company has substantial growth projects ahead.

EQT Corporation's production performance has continued to be at the high end of guidance and the company is doing that while managing capex. The company had $134 million of class action expenses in the quarter so true annualized FCF would be more ~5% yield. The company is continuing to grow taking advantage of long-term demand.

Overview

EQT Corporation is a large, domestic, and vertically integrated natural gas producer with substantial opportunity.

The company has a $44 billion enterprise volume with substantial upstream production at a mere ~$2 / MMBtu across a multi-decade de-risked inventory. And then strong integrated volumes through midstream and downstream opportunities. The company had $8 billion in debt with a $5 billion long-term debt target, so it has some way to go, but it's in a great position.

Operations Change

EQT Corporation is continuing to grow with less capital across a number of exciting assets.

The company has ramped up its project count by more than 50% at 15-20% less capital and its wells are performing better for longer, with realized uplift seeing 20+% outperformance. The company acquired Equitrans last year, and it's continuing to benefit from synergies between the two companies as an integrated company.

The company is ramping up its feet per day of efficiency and it expects to have further room to improve from its current levels. A 10% decrease in costs per well will enable the company to better manage its well costs in what's a complicated and expensive industry to operate in with volatile MMBtu costs for the company.

The company's strategic portfolio improvements will enable continued future growth. EQT Corporation expects to see ~$250 million in additional forecast annual FCF by 2029, representing a strong growth rate, and the company has substantial opportunities to grow additional production in Appalachia. This includes a number of power stations and midstream pipelines.

These are assets that will see long-term multi-decade demand once they're built and EQT Corporation will be well positioned to benefit.

From 2025-2029 at current $3.2 / mmbtu prices, the company expects to generate roughly $13 billion in FCF. That's an 8% annualized FCF yield at current MMBTU prices. However, we wouldn't consider current prices particularly strong, as we'll see below, there's a number of growth opportunities that we expect will keep prices high.

Growth Opportunities

The company has two substantial growth opportunities for natural gas, datacenters and LNG.

Natural gas is a strong and cheap form of electricity and it's something the United States has in spades. As datacenter demand is growing substantially, gigawatts of new power demand is leading to new natural gas demand. More importantly, EQT Corporation is well positioned for some of the largest new datacenters, with Virginia a dominant new datacenter state.

~55 GW of announced datacenters are in EQT's core operating area with ~10 GW under construction implying a potential opportunity of 7.5 Bcf/d in new demand.

Another massive opportunity is LNG where the U.S. is quickly growing and is now the largest exporter of LNG in the world with a massive 18 Bcf/day in service. A new wave of capacity is expected to come close to doubling LNG demand and this is substantial multi-decade demand that forms a significant source of baseload natural gas demand.

EQT Corporation is well positioned to take advantage of this.

EQT Corporation Shareholder Returns

EQT Corporation has the ability to manage its balance sheet and generate substantial cash flow for returns. For perspective recent strip prices are ~$3.5+ / MMBtu.

The company expects that by the end of next year it'll be able to drop to net debt of less than $5 billion, although it can handle a wide variety of scenarios. That's substantial debt improvements from where the company was in 2024, and we expect that EQT Corporation will be able to continue improving its debt outlook.

Once it drops below $5 billion, the company has the interest expenditures, etc., to focus on alternative shareholder returns.

EQT Corporation expects that cumulate FCF will be roughly $3 billion / year at current prices over the next 5-years, which is a high single-digit FCF yield. After balance sheet improvements, that leaves the company with substantial room for additional shareholder returns. The company is well positioned versus its peers at various natural gas prices.

That's because the company now has an integrated end-to-end asset portfolio with midstream assets that enable it to maintain robust cash flow.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is that EQT Corporation is fundamentally reliant on natural gas prices. Building out production with a downturn in datacenter construction or LNG construction could push down natural gas prices substantially and result in the company coming in at the lower end of its FCF guidance, which wouldn't be sufficient FCF to justify investing.

Conclusion

EQT Corporation is a company in the right place at the right time. The newly integrated company has a strong portfolio of assets, and it's well positioned near major datacenter construction and LNG growth. Virginia is a datacenter hub, and most LNG production is on the Gulf Coast with massive pipeline connections.

The company can generate a high single-digit FCF yield at current strip prices, and there's a number of projects that will support higher for longer cash flow. The company's net debt is well positioned, and the company can generate substantial shareholder returns, making it a valuable investment for a changing market.

data.title: BlackRock Total Return V.I. Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
• The fund posted a return of 1.57% (Class I shares) for the second quarter of 2025.
  • Structured products, U.S. high yield credit, and duration positioning (management of interest rate sensitivity) contributed to performance, while the macro strategies detracted.
  • The fund increased its overweight position in top-line duration, adding to its overweight exposure to front-end U.S. rates. The overweight allocation to agency mortgage-backed securities was increased and the underweight position in U.S. investment grade credit was maintained. We continued to favor high-quality securitized assets and increased exposure to U.S. high yield credit.
Contributors Detractors
The allocation to securitized products, particularly non-agency and commercial mortgage-backed securities, contributed as spreads broadly tightened. Similarly, U.S. high yield credit was helpful as spreads retracted following the bout of spread widening due to the “Liberation Day” tariff announcements. The fund’s duration position also contributed, as U.S. front-end rates rallied while the “belly” and long end of the yield curve sold off. At the same time, European front-end rates rallied in reaction to concerns about geopolitics and slowing growth. The macro strategies, particularly hedges on equity exposures, detracted from performance.

Further insight

During the quarter, the fund increased its overweight duration exposure (5.98 years versus the benchmark’s 5.89 years), maintaining a steepening bias. We increased our “steepener” (positioning that seeks to benefit from a steepening of the yield curve, where the difference between long-term and short-term yields increases), adding to the front end of the yield curve, which we viewed as attractive given elevated all-in yields and potential hedge benefits. Simultaneously, we increased our underweight exposure to the belly and long end of the yield curve, anticipating weakness in these segments due to fiscal deficit concerns and term premium expansion. In spread sectors, we tactically rotated across sectors as we continued to look for attractive, high-quality sources of income while we remained cautious down the credit stack.

data.title: Arrive AI: Not All AI And Drone Stocks Are A Buy
data.description:
Currently drones and artificial intelligence are attracting significant attention from potential customers as well as investors. One of the companies that wants to capitalize on this is Arrive AI (NASDAQ:ARAI), a developer of docking stations for autonomous delivery vehicles. In this report, I discuss the company’s activities and the risks faced by the company.

Arrive AI: Smart Logistics Hub Backed By Patents

Arrive AI provides Mailbox-as-a-Service, or MaaS, for secure delivery and pick up of parcels by autonomous ground and delivery robots. Essentially, the company provides a pick up and drop off station for delivery drones. The company’s main product right now it Arrive Point pictured above. It is not a simple mailbox, as it is temperature controlled making it useful for delivery and pick up of pharmaceuticals and also ensuring the ability for delivery vehicles to access it regardless of weather conditions. I consider the Arrive Point more of a demonstrator with early limited commercial appeal. The reason is that if drone deliveries do take off, this would be more applicable as a smart mailbox for the receiving party but as a pickup point I do believe that a larger version is needed to facilitate multi-drone operations.

As the name of the company suggests AI is being leveraged. The company’s Arrive Points use AI for smart security, environment control and security. Furthermore, its autonomous last-mile platform is a software layer orchestrating workflows and runs analytics backed by AI.

I believe that the concept is promising, and Arrive AI also has some partnerships:

  • Go2 Delivery; a Virginia-based courier, to autonomously deliver high-risk, high-value specialty pharmacy products using Arrive AI technology.
  • Skye Air Mobility; Skye Air is India’s dominant and rapidly expanding hyperlocal drone delivery platform, making about 6,000 deliveries per day. Under the terms of the agreement, the two companies will collaborate to deploy up to 500 Arrive Points in India, including the capital of New Delhi. The first Arrive Point units are expected to be delivered to Skye Air by the end of 2025.

In July, the company also started a hiring initiative to triple its workforce by adding 40 members. It shows the extremely small scale that the company has been operating on to-date and that also means that the scalability of the business and potential pricing of products and services is unproven, and that provides as clear risk. A positive is that in August, the FAA cleared the path for drone deliveries. It should be noticed that this is not a direct clearance but proposed rulemaking.

First Revenues For Arrive AI

In the second quarter, Arrive AI booked its first revenues. The company derives its revenues through three streams:

  1. Consulting services, which brought $89,000 in revenues and it the early phases to help mature a hyperlocal delivery network this could provide a significant revenue opportunity.
  2. Installation fees which brought in $15,000. These are project-based revenues related to the installation of the Arrive Points.
  3. Subscriptions, which brought in $225 during the quarter.

The mature business set up likely is the combination of lower margin sales of the Arrive AI points with recurring revenues from subscriptions at higher margins. We note that the bulk of the revenues did not come from the hardware installation and subscription services. For Arrive AI rolling out more Arrive Points is going to be critical, not only to their recurring revenues but also to their gross margins on installation.

The net loss widened year-on-year from $1.46 million to $4.68 million but this included $3 million in costs related to the direct listing on the Nasdaq Stock Exchange. So, the losses would have been roughly similar year-over-year.

The major challenge for the company is that as it hires more workers its actual cash costs are going to rise. So we keep an eye on the general and administrative expenses. We also note that sales and marketing expenses and R&D declined significantly. This was driven by a TV commercial campaign last year and a non-recurring project nature for engineering. I can only hope that the company does not pour significant funds in TV commercial campaigns for the simple reason that the market right now actually starts with businesses, not with consumers and TV commercials tend to be expensive.

The company had an operating cash burn of $3.8 million in H1 or $7.6 million annualized and investing activities of $47,827. So, annualized the cash burn is around $7.5-$8 million compared to $2.4-$3 million in 2023 and 2024. The company raised $4 million from the issuance of convertible debt and $1 million from issuing common stock and warrant exercises. Proceeds were used to pay off existing notes of $4,187 and part of its deferred offering costs and cover the cash burn leaving the company with $0.6075 million in cash. So, the main issue for the company is the cash position and it will require the company to continue issuing convertible notes which are highly likely to be converted to common stock and dilute shareholders.

A Look At The Listing And Capital Structure

The company went public through a direct listing. Essentially, this means that it went public without raising capital. It does help the company securing fresh funds such as the tranches of the $40 million capital infusion by Streeterville Capital. Streeterville provides the capital in the form of an equity line. It should be noted that there are several conditions for the funding. The most important ones are that in case of converting debt to equity, Streeterville Capital’s ownership cannot exceed 10%.

In that case, the company would basically have to either pay off the debt in cash or issue shares raising capital from other investors and subsequently convert the debt to equity. Otherwise, it is unlikely that any remainder of the $40 million would be accessible to Arrive AI. Furthermore, if the share price falls below $0.25, Arrive AI needs to repay in the equity line in cash. Latest figures available show that Streeterville Capital already owns 9.1% of the shares.

Another condition is that the company can only draw on the equity line as long as the market cap remains aboved $100 million. The current market cap is around $167 million. Streeterville gets stock against the Fixed Price of $4 per share as long as the market cap is above $250 million, which is not the cash now. When the market cap is below $250 million on average for 3-days, Streeterville can obtain shares at the fixed price of $4 or 90% of the lowest volume weighted average price over the prior 10 days.

The company secured a second tranche in August of roughly $4 million. This puts the pro-forma cash position at $4.6 million, which, at the current cash burn rate funds the company for another quarter.

My concern is that Streeterville will sooner or later hit the 10% cap, which seems like a dilution cap but in reality it would accelerate dilution for the simple reason that in order to keep access to the funding the company would also need to raise funds from the capital market as raising through the conventional debt market is unlikely. It should be noted that the $40 million spread over several tranches is not convertible debt. Convertible debt could be settled with shares at maturity or at an exercisable date. The agreement between Arrive AI and Streeterville is a pre-paid purchase agreement where the company basically issues shares against the tranches provided by Streeterville.

Alternatively, Streeterville can sell its shares at a gain and then proceed with the next tranche of funding. This would essentially create a carousel where Streeterville capitalizes on the spread between the Fixed Price and the current trading price while the incremental funding remains accessible for Arrive AI. However, for other shareholders it does induce a significant risk of downside on the share price.

Conclusion: Arrive AI Is Too High Of A Risk

The concept of a docking station in my view could work; however, I also note that a single dock like we have seen on the picture is not the long-term scalable solution. Perhaps for testing, pilots and thinly populated areas as well as an initial launch a single dock could work. However, scaling will likely require the development of a stack or rack of Arrive Points. That is not the biggest issue. The main issue is that the revenue generation model makes sense but is unproved and the scalability of the business as a result is also unproven. Furthermore, the company is currently only funded towards the end of the year with cash burns likely to continue. Streeterville already owns 9.1% of the shares and for the full $40 million to remain accessible, Arrive AI likely will have to issue stock to other investors. Effectively the 10% maximum cap for Streeterville at this point does not limit dilution risk fully and it may in fact require Arrive AI to raise capital such the ownership for Streeterville remains under 10%.

So, Arrive AI has capital needs and opted for direct listing which means the company did not raise capital through the listing itself. The Streeterville Capital $40 million infusion seems to be a gamechanger. However, the way it is structured it is essentially a bridge that is only accessible to the company if the stock and equity base is strong enough meaning that additional capital needs to be raised by issuing stock. As a result, I consider the dilution risk to be too high and mark the stock a strong sell.

data.title: Northern Arizona Tax-Exempt Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Global markets contended with a turbulent macroeconomic landscape, largely shaped by evolving U.S. policy initiatives. The tariffs announced on April 2 (“Liberation Day”) were broader and more substantial than anticipated, delivering a sharp initial shock to sentiment. However, incremental de-escalation efforts, including 90-day pauses and an emphasis on trade negotiations, helped stabilize the situation. Despite these efforts, the persistence of elevated tariffs and policy uncertainty continued to reshape the global economic environment. Consensus forecasts, led by U.S. revisions, shaved global growth expectations and pushed inflation projections higher. Although one-year U.S. recession probabilities moderated after the initial spike, they remain elevated at roughly 35-40%. Global economic activity, while distorted by trade data and front-loading ahead of tariffs, proved relatively resilient. Inflation pressures were subdued across several major economies, but core inflation rates stayed above central bank targets and U.S. inflation is expected to rise in the second half of the year due to tariff effects. Geopolitical risk also pushed its way towards center stage in the second quarter. In addition to the ongoing Ukraine war, tensions escalated in the Middle East and India-Pakistan. Even as the U.S. launched airstrikes against nuclear sites in Iran, a ceasefire was agreed to within two weeks of the initial Israel strikes. Oil prices gained (and subsequently fell following the ceasefire) about 15%. The episode was largely without sustained major financial market impacts as energy infrastructure and global oil shipments were not meaningfully targeted. The Federal Reserve Board adopted a cautious stance, holding rates steady during the quarter, though markets are pricing in several rate cuts over the coming year.

Financial markets performed well in the second quarter with gains across most major asset classes. Equity markets proved quite resilient in the face of considerable uncertainty throughout the quarter across a number of avenues including U.S. tariff policy, geopolitics and fiscal policy. Meanwhile, the U.S. dollar initially weakened in April and then continued to slide lower throughout the rest of the quarter as some investors looked to diversify away from the U.S. Broader dollar indexes were down over 6%. Fixed income returns were positive. Credit spreads tightened in the quarter while longer-term interest rates were modestly higher, resulting in a more favorable setup for high yield fixed income versus investment grade.

Amid shifting fiscal and inflation dynamics, long-term sovereign bond yields pushed higher across several major countries. The Liberation Day trade shock led longer-dated Treasury yields 45-50 basis points (0.45%, 0.50%) higher in just five days. During this period there was an unsettling break from historical patterns, including equity losses and dollar weakening, but de-escalatory tariff policies eventually helped provide stability. Front-end yields ended the quarter lower as the market priced in a delayed but more aggressive Federal Reverse Board rate cutting cycle.

The Municipal sector struggled during the quarter and underperformed taxable fixed income. The Bloomberg Municipal Index returned -0.12% on the quarter. The AAA Municipal yield curve continued to regain normal shape. Following Liberation Day, municipal yields rose 83-87 basis points (0.83%, 0.87%) in 3 days dramatically underperforming treasuries. Yields recovered from the initial shock, but long-term rates remained more elevated. The Municipal yield curve closed the quarter with 189 basis points (1.89%) of steepness, the highest since Summer 2017. Overall, the 2-year yield fell by 0.05% while 10, 20 and 30-year yields rose by 0.01%, 0.20%, and 0.26%, respectively. Municipal credit spreads also widened during the quarter reflecting the elevated policy risks. On an absolute basis, lower rated bonds widened the most. Single-A and BBB-rated spreads were wider by 7 and 10 basis points (0.07%, 0.10%). Despite the modest widening, spreads remain tight versus historic averages. Municipal new issue supply logged a record quarter with over an estimated $160 billion pricing. This outpaces the previous second quarter record set in 2008 by 10%. Fund flows coupled with record principal and coupon payments helped to absorb the heavy issuance. Elevated yields and attractive pricing brought $7.3 billion in fund flows to the municipal market in May and June, reversing the nearly $7 billion in outflows logged in April. The flows paired with heavy principal and income reinvestment helped buoy bond prices the heavy supply environment. Arizona bonds generally performed in line with the broader Investment Grade Municipal Index during the quarter.

Explanation of Results

The Fund underperformed its benchmark in the quarter. Curve positioning was the largest detractor as the fund was overweight longer duration bonds as the curve steepened. Credit allocation helped to offset some of the impact of curve. An underweight to the BBB segment was additive.

data.title: Northern Active M International Equity Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Non-U.S. developed markets, as measured by the MSCI World Ex U.S. IM Index, advanced 12.70% in the second quarter. European Central Bank easing policy, increased government stimulus, a weaker U.S. dollar, and a recently improved corporate earnings picture, were a tailwind for international equity market performance. Europe slightly outperformed the Pacific region, though both saw strong returns for the quarter (MSCI Europe +11.38%, MSCI Pacific +12.30%). Japan trailed the Pacific region, gaining 11.366%. Hong Kong stocks up 15.80% were the best performing in the region. All sectors were positive except for energy which was down 1.50% as Middle East conflict caused large swings in oil prices. It was a strong quarter for momentum and growth factors, as widespread improved investor sentiment led to more risk- on sentiment in international markets. International small caps outperformed, as the MSCI World ex USA Small Cap Index gained 16.93%, while the MSCI World ex USA Index was up 12.03%.

The Active M International Fund outperformed in the second quarter on strong stock selection across countries and sectors. Exposure to aerospace, defense and engineering stocks in Germany, France and the U.K., benefited from government proposals to increase spending on defense and infrastructure. Holdings in financials within Europe added value along with Internet names in Sweden. Healthcare stocks, specifically biotech outsourcing holdings within Ireland, detracted. Exposure across several emerging market countries contributed. This included defense equipment manufacturers and financials in South Korea, semiconductor companies in Taiwan and fintech, and traditional banks in Brazil. The Fund’s Chinese internet consumer market place and rideshare holdings weighed on results. Causeway, a sub-adviser with a value orientation, underperformed, largely due to weak stock selection in Japan, a result of exposure to factory automation holdings and being underweight semiconductor related companies. Sub-adviser Wellington was also challenged within Japan but added value in the U.K., France, Italy and Ireland; and financials in South Korea and Brazil. WCM, the quality growth-oriented sub-adviser, added value through aerospace and defense stocks in various markets including France, Germany, Japan and South Korea.

data.title: NVDY: Good Option For Risk Averse Investors But Gains Could Be Capped
data.description:
Introduction

The YieldMax NVDY Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:NVDY) is a fund that aims to provide income and some upside on the shares of the underlying equity, Nvidia (NVDA). This is achieved through writing and selling options on NVDA. Please see below for some details of the fund, taken from the prospectus.

Expense Ratio: 0.99%

Net Assets: $1.91B

Volume: 254K

We believe that investing in NVDA is one of the purest play ways to get exposure to the AI computing sector, and in the following section, will review their latest earnings and other relevant news that could move the shares.

NVDA Earnings and News

The firm recently reported earnings, and it was a report that most of the investment community was on the lookout for as a bellwether of how the AI trade and investment cycle has been going. Per their earnings, sales surged 56% in the quarter to $46.74B, which was slightly above consensus estimates of $46.06B. Adjusted EPS came in at $1.05, which was also slightly above the $1.01 estimate from analysts. NVDA provided guidance for the upcoming quarter for sales of $54B, and the key takeaway on that figure is that all China related estimates are not included. This means that all sales of H20 shipments are not in the forecast, which is a positive in our view because of all of the political uncertainty that is surrounding the H20 chip at the moment. Please see the image below for a detailed summary of their latest earnings report.

In our view, this earnings call showed that there is still significant traction in the AI trend, and we believe that this market is only going to continue growing and NVDA should remain a strong beneficiary of this wave of continued investment. There is the possibility that the Chinese market remains closed to the company, which is why we are pleased to see all China related guidance removed from the print. This should alleviate investor concerns over basing forecasts and models off of China numbers that may or may not come to fruition. Overall, this earnings call gives us confidence that the AI investment theme should continue to dominate through the rest of the year and that calls for AI being in a bubble may be an overly bearish view.

Another piece of news that we believe should help move the stock along in the upcoming quarter is the confirmation of additional share buybacks that have been authorized by the company. NVDA has announced plans to buy back $60B worth of shares, which is in addition to the existing $14.7B remaining on its existing repurchase agreement that has already been taking place. This buying momentum from the firm signifies confidence that its shares are still under or less than fairly valued from management’s perspective, which should give the broader investment community faith in where the company’s stock can go from here. We believe that their strong earnings and their buyback plans are going to bolster NVDA shares through the end of the year.

Fund Strategy

We did a deep dive into NVDY’s options strategy in our prior analysis so we will do a more abbreviated exploration here. We recommend checking out our other article for a more detailed deep dive. In order to understand the covered call strategy we will look at the same scenario:

  • We own 100 shares of NVDA purchased at $100 per share

  • The current price of NVDA is $100

  • There is a $115 strike call option that we sell at a premium of $300.

The below chart visualizes this scenario

The blue line represents your returns if you just owned the 100 shares while the red line represents your returns if you owned the shares and sold the call option. We notice a few features of the chart:

  • For generally lower prices of NVDA, the options strategy (red line) is higher than the pure equity strategy

  • After NVDA’s price increases past a certain point ($118), the returns from the pure equity strategy surpass the options strategy.

  • After NVDA’s price increases past a certain point ($115), the returns from the options strategy become flat and no longer move up or down as the price increases.

Why does the chart behave this way? Let’s break it down.

At prices lower than $115, the two lines are parallel but the options strategy is higher by $300. This is due to the premium we earned from selling the call option. So for lower prices of NVDA, the options strategy serves to reduce potential losses.

At the $115 mark, the options strategy line flattens. This is because the strike price of the option is $115. At this point or any price above, whoever bought the option has incentive to exercise the option and buy the shares of NVDA from you for $115 each. This is why the line flattens - we assume that the option was exercised and you no longer own the outstanding option or the shares.

At the $118 point, the two strategies have equal returns. This is caused by a combination of the strike price of $115 and the premium of $300. If we did not get any premium for selling the call, the 2 lines would intersect at the strike price of $115. However since we did get a premium of $3 per share, the intersection point increases by $3 to $118.

The Takeaways

So what does this strategy help with? We see that as long as NVDA stays below $118, we are better off selling the option but if NVDA increases by too much we would have made more money by not selling the option.

Given that, we see that the covered call strategy is good for investors who believe NVDA will not increase significantly. (The definition of “significantly” changes based on the options sold, prices of NVDA and the premiums. In this specific scenario, we are expecting NVDA to not increase more than 18% to $118).

This strategy also helps reduce losses if NVDA loses value. However, if you believe NVDA will lose value, then you are better off just selling your shares rather than selling a covered call.

Putting those 2 points together, this strategy is best for investors who believe NVDA will:

  1. Not decrease in price

  2. Not increase significantly

Risks

You will not own shares of NVDA directly

For a standard covered calls strategy the fund will buy the shares and sell the call options. As an investor in the fund, you will not have direct ownership over those shares of NVDA so you will have less control of when it is bought/sold and you will not get other benefits such as voting rights. NVDY actually does not even buy the shares of NVDA directly but instead trades options in a way that mimics the price movement of NVDA. This makes your ownership of NVDA even more indirect and could introduce additional risk from options trading.

If you are interested in the covered call strategy but also want to be directly exposed to NVDA and the rights that come with ownership of those shares, we do not recommend this fund. If you are experienced enough with options trading, you may also opt to sell covered calls yourself.

NVDA has moved significantly up in the past few years

As one of the biggest names in AI, NVDA shares have benefitted greatly from the recent trends in large language models. This could be seen as both a positive and a negative point for the covered calls strategy. On one hand, if the company has shown large positive movements in the recent past, it will mean that option premiums are higher and you will get more income from selling the calls. On the other hand, it could also mean that NVDA increases by more than you hope and your covered call strategy ends up losing money (relative to just owning the shares).

Conclusion

NVDA remains a leader in the AI investment theme, and their latest earnings shows that this trend is not stopping anytime soon. Further, their large share buyback program leads us to believe that management still views shares as less than fairly valued, and that future growth is still possible.

The covered call strategy that NVDY employs may not be for everyone but it can definitely add value if executed correctly. If you are a more risk averse investor who still wants some exposure to NVDA growth, then NVDY could be a great fund for you. If you are instead less risk averse and want to maximize gains, the covered call strategy is likely not a good option for you.

Given how the fund can be a good or bad option depending on your investing style, we rate NVDY as a hold.

data.title: Northern Active M Emerging Markets Equity Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Emerging markets, as measured by the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, appreciated by +11.99% in the second quarter. The top performing countries were Korea (+32.71%), Greece (+29.59%), and Taiwan (+26.09%). The largest contributors were Taiwan (+4.25%), South Korea (+2.96%) and India (+9.22%). Many emerging market countries showed resilient domestic growth and contained inflation in the period. India’s steady economic expansion and Brazil’s falling interest rates improved investor sentiment, leading to broad gains. Surging 'artificial intelligence' and 'high performance computing' semiconductor chips demand, supported semiconductor stocks in Taiwan and South Korea. Although value delivered another positive quarter, the MSCI Emerging Markets Growth Index outperformed, with a return of +13.82%, compared to the MSCI Emerging Markets Value Index's return of +10.02%. Small cap stocks surpassed their larger cap counterparts (MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index advanced +17.17% versus +10.73% for the MSCI Emerging Markets Large Cap Index).

The Active M Emerging Markets Equity Fund slightly outperformed in the second quarter. Country selection was the main contributor while stock selection detracted. An underweight to China was one of the largest contributors, though stock selection in China was also beneficial. An underweight to Saudi Arabia helped, as did selection in industrials names in Brazil. An overweight to Mexico and Greece added incremental value relative to the broader index. Meanwhile, an underweight to a Taiwanese semiconductor name was detrimental. Weak stock selection in India, South Africa and the United Arab Emirates also detracted. Sub-adviser Fidelity Institutional Asset Management (FIAM LLC) trailed due to weak stock selection in Brazil, China, Taiwan and Turkey but added value through stock selection in South Africa and Greece, an overweight to Greece, an underweight to India and void Saudi Arabia. Sub-adviser Axiom outperformed with strong stock selection in China and South Korea and an overweight to Greece. Sub-adviser Westwood outperformed as a result of strong stock selection in Brazil, an underweight to China and an overweight and strong stock selection in Mexico, while an underweight to Taiwan was detrimental as was stock selection in South Korea.
